title: Adding a honey super
subtitle: When and how to put extra storage space on a colony so bees have room to work and do not swarm from congestion.
excerpt: A colony that runs out of room above the brood box may swarm from congestion alone. Adding a super at the right moment, not too early but before the colony is cramped, is one of the simplest swarm-prevention tools. This covers the timing cues, the queen-excluder check, and how to position drawn versus new frames.
sourceNotes:
National Bee Unit, Beekeeper's Toolkit (2024 edition), section on honey supers and swarm prevention. https://www.nationalbeeunit.com
Frank Pearson, Bee-keeping for All (1923), Chapter X on honey production, Internet Archive.

glossaryTerms.definition: The shallower box placed above the brood chamber where bees store surplus honey for harvest. One or two supers sit above a queen excluder so the queen does not lay in the honey frames.
A perforated zinc or plastic grid between the brood box and the first super. The gaps let workers through but are too small for the larger queen's thorax.
Comb that bees have already built out from the wax foundation into full depth cells. Far more attractive to bees than fresh foundation, and will be filled faster.
A wax sheet pressed with a hexagonal cell pattern, fitted into a frame to give bees the starting surface for comb building.

A sticky resinous substance bees collect from tree buds and bark and use to seal gaps in the hive, coat surfaces, and glue frames. Sets hard in cold weather; becomes sticky in warm weather. Stains skin and clothing dark brown.

content.text: The right moment to add a

is when bees are occupying eight or nine of the brood frames and beginning to crowd the top bars during a routine inspection. At that point the colony has the numbers to work a second box and the population pressure that leads to swarming if there is nowhere to expand.

During your spring inspection, count how many frames are covered with bees. When eight or more frames are well covered and there is brood on most of them, add the first super that week. Do not wait for the hive to feel congested: by the time it does, the colony may already be making swarm preparations.
Check or fit the queen excluder first
Before adding the super box, check that the

is clean and undamaged. Hold it up to the light: you should see uniform-sized slots with no warping or bent wires that would let the queen through. A flat zinc excluder that has been stepped on can have distorted bars that miss the queen.
from a previous season, use it. Bees move into drawn comb immediately; undrawn

may sit untouched for a fortnight while the colony decides whether to work it. If all you have is foundation, position it in the centre of the super box rather than at the edges, bees work inwards from the brood cluster, and they will start drawing the middle frames first.
Use smoke as for any hive visit. Lay the excluder across the brood box so the bars run across the frames (not along them, to prevent
bridges from blocking worker passage). Set the super box on top, level and flush. Replace the crown board and roof. Note the date and the colony's frame coverage in your apiary log.

attrs.title: When to add a second super
attrs.body: Add the next super when bees are working six or seven of the frames in the first super. Either place it above the first (bees move up naturally) or below the first super and above the excluder to encourage faster uptake of new foundation. The 'below' method brings the new box closer to the cluster and tends to get foundation drawn faster in early spring.
